strategy_research:
{
  "strategies": [
    {
      "name": "Dual Momentum – Global Equity Momentum (GEM)",
      "description": "Combines relative momentum (SPY vs EFA each month) with absolute momentum (12-month return vs T-bills). Rotates 100% into the stronger equity index, or flees to AGG/TLT when absolute momentum is negative. ~2-3 trades per year.",
      "evidence": [
        "Antonacci, G. (2012). 'Dual Momentum Investing' – NAAIM Founders Award winner 2012",
        "Antonacci, G. (2013). 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum' – SSRN",
        "AllocateSmartly composite backtest 1974–2023: Sharpe 1.02, UPI 3.10",
        "QuantifiedStrategies backtest: CAGR 6.75%, max drawdown 30% vs S&P 500 drawdown 55%"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.80–1.02 (period-dependent; composite version ~1.02; simple GEM ~0.80)",
      "market_conditions": "Excels in trending bear markets and multi-year bull runs with clear leadership. Underperforms in whipsaw/choppy markets and sharp V-shaped recoveries (e.g., 2020 rebound, 2022 was -29.87% worst year). Vulnerable to abrupt regime changes.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "TLT"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "low"
    },
    {
      "name": "Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA-G4)",
      "description": "Breadth-momentum strategy by Keller & Keuning. Scores SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG monthly using 1/3/6/12-month weighted momentum. If all 4 have positive momentum, holds the top scorer 100%. If any is negative, shifts partially or fully into best defensive asset (SHY/IEF/LQD). Aggressive crash protection.",
      "evidence": [
        "Keller, W.J. & Keuning, J.W. (2017). 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation' – SSRN #3002624",
        "AllocateSmartly review: out-of-sample VAA-G4 Sharpe 0.92, CAGR 16%, max drawdown -10%",
        "PortfolioDB G4 Aggressive: confirmed out-of-sample persistence post-publication"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.92–0.98 out-of-sample (VAA-G4); in-sample 1.24 (G12 variant)",
      "market_conditions": "Excellent crash protection – fast momentum weighting exits markets quickly. Underperforms in strong, uninterrupted bull markets due to high cash allocations. High turnover creates tax drag in taxable accounts.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "IWM"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "SPY-TLT Universal Investment Strategy (UIS)",
      "description": "Dynamically allocates between SPY and TLT each month by finding the ratio that maximized the modified Sharpe ratio over the trailing lookback period. Exploits the negative stock-bond correlation to produce equity-like returns with bond-like volatility. Always fully invested.",
      "evidence": [
        "Seeking Alpha 20-year backtest: Sharpe 1.28 vs SPY alone 0.50 and TLT alone 0.79",
        "Alvarez Quant Trading SPY-TLT rotation analysis confirming structural negative correlation benefit",
        "Exploits documented flight-to-quality effect (Ilmanen 2011, 'Expected Returns')"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "1.28 (20-year backtest, monthly rebalancing)",
      "market_conditions": "Relies on negative stock-bond correlation, which held 2000–2021 but broke down in 2022 (both fell simultaneously during the rate-hiking cycle). Best in environments where bonds provide genuine equity hedging.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "low"
    },
    {
      "name": "Naïve Risk Parity (Inverse Volatility Weighting)",
      "description": "Weights SPY, GLD, TLT, and AGG inversely proportional to their trailing 20-day volatility, so each asset contributes equally to total portfolio risk. Rebalances monthly. No leverage required for small accounts. True diversification across uncorrelated risk premia.",
      "evidence": [
        "Qian, E. (2005). 'Risk Parity Portfolios' – PanAgora Asset Management white paper",
        "Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen (2012). 'Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity' – Financial Analysts Journal",
        "S&P Risk Parity Indices study: Sharpe ratio higher than 60/40 across all volatility targets tested",
        "QuantInsti backtest: Risk Parity Sharpe 1.57 vs equal-weight 1.07, lower max drawdown"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "1.07–1.57 (unlevered, multi-asset; period-dependent)",
      "market_conditions": "Strong in normal regimes with low cross-asset correlation. Fails when all assets fall together (2022 rate shock, Q1 2020 initial COVID crash). Bonds-heavy allocation can drag returns in rising rate environments.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "Meb Faber GTAA – 10-Month Moving Average Trend Following",
      "description": "Allocates equally across up to 5 asset classes (domestic stocks, international stocks, bonds, commodities/gold, REITs). Each position is held only when its price is above its 10-month SMA; otherwise, that slice moves to cash/T-bills. Monthly rebalancing. Based on 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation' (SSRN).",
      "evidence": [
        "Faber, M. (2007). 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation' – SSRN #962461 (10,000+ citations)",
        "Faber (2013 revisit): 1973–2012 backtest: equity-like returns with bond-like drawdowns",
        "3-asset SPY/TLT/GLD version CAGR 8.9%, max drawdown only 18%, from 2006",
        "CFM Research: trend following Sharpe 0.72 over 200 years across all asset sectors"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.50–0.70 (5-asset version, monthly rebalancing outperforms annual by significant margin)",
      "market_conditions": "Excels in prolonged trends and bear markets – avoids most of 2000–2002, 2008. Underperforms in trendless/choppy markets and during 2010–2019 pure US equity bull run where cash drag hurt. Simple and highly transparent.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "low"
    },
    {
      "name": "Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA) – Canary Universe",
      "description": "Extension of VAA by Keller & Keuning. Uses a 'canary' universe (VWO + BND) as early warning indicators. If either canary has negative momentum, defensive allocation kicks in across 12 offensive ETFs scored by 13612W momentum. Balances aggression with robust crash protection.",
      "evidence": [
        "Keller, W.J. & Keuning, J.W. (2018). 'Breadth Momentum and the Canary Universe: DAA' – SSRN #3212862",
        "TuringTrader backtest: strong risk-adjusted returns across full 50-year test",
        "QuantConnect live test: gained +5% during Q1 2020 crash while S&P fell ~34%",
        "Monte Carlo analysis confirms statistically significant drawdown reduction vs buy-and-hold"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.90–1.10 (in-sample; out-of-sample slightly lower due to turnover costs)",
      "market_conditions": "Exceptional bear market / crash protection. The canary universe triggers defensive mode before large drawdowns. Weaker in melt-up bull markets where cash allocation creates drag. High turnover is a concern in taxable accounts.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "200-Day Moving Average Trend Filter on SPY",
      "description": "Simplest possible trend-following rule: hold SPY when price is above 200-day SMA, shift to TLT or AGG (safe haven) when below. Monthly or daily check. Avoids major drawdowns by exiting equity during sustained downtrends. Documented to exit before 2001, 2008, and 2020 bear markets.",
      "evidence": [
        "Faber, M. (2007 SSRN): 200-day MA Sharpe 0.704 vs S&P 500 buy-and-hold 0.471 since 1951",
        "CFM (2015). 'Trend Following: A Persistent Market Anomaly' – 200-year Sharpe 0.72",
        "Seeking Alpha backtest 1951–present: annualized 7.11% at σ=10.1% vs 7.24% at σ=15.37%",
        "Fidelity study: S&P 500 returns 11.9% annualized above 200-day MA, -4.3% below"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.70–0.72 (vs ~0.47 for buy-and-hold over same period since 1951)",
      "market_conditions": "Works well in trending markets and recessions. Significantly underperforms in V-shaped corrections and sideways/whipsaw environments. Generates false signals in volatile, trendless conditions. Low ~4–6 trades per year limits transaction costs.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "daily",
      "complexity": "low"
    },
    {
      "name": "Volatility-Targeted Multi-Asset Portfolio",
      "description": "Scales the allocation between a risky portfolio (e.g., 60% SPY / 40% TLT) up or down based on realized 20-day volatility relative to a target (e.g., 10% annualized). When volatility is low, leverage slightly up; when high, reduce exposure and hold cash. Targets consistent risk, not constant notional.",
      "evidence": [
        "Moreira & Muir (2017). 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios' – Journal of Finance: higher Sharpe for US equity",
        "Bongaerts, Kang & van Dijk (2020). 'Conditional Volatility Targeting' – Financial Analysts Journal: consistently boosts Sharpe and reduces tail risk",
        "Research Affiliates (2021). 'Harnessing Volatility Targeting in Multi-Asset Portfolios': improves Sharpe for risk assets and balanced portfolios",
        "Harvey et al. (2018): volatility targeting reduces tail risks, Sharpe gains concentrated in equity/credit"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.80–1.10 for equity/balanced portfolios (conditional version; unconditional results are mixed)",
      "market_conditions": "Benefits from volatility clustering – works best when volatility is persistent. Underperforms in the 2009–2019 low-vol bull market. Struggles with sudden volatility spikes (e.g., March 2020) where the model reacts too slowly.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "daily",
      "complexity": "medium"
    }
  ],
  "recommendation": "Start with Dual Momentum GEM or VAA-G4. Both are monthly-rebalanced, require only 1–4 ETFs from your available universe, and have the strongest out-of-sample academic support. GEM is the simplest (2 rules, ~2 trades/year, Sharpe ~0.80–1.02) and is ideal for a $1,000 account with minimal transaction friction. VAA-G4 offers better crash protection and a higher out-of-sample Sharpe (~0.92) at the cost of modestly higher turnover. For moderate risk tolerance, GEM is the first implementation: each month check if SPY > EFA on 12-month return (relative momentum), then check if the winner beats T-bills (absolute momentum). If yes, buy the winner. If no, hold AGG. The strategy is academically robust, fully rules-based, avoids subjective judgment, and has demonstrated lower drawdowns (–30% vs –55% for S&P 500) across 50 years of data — giving superior risk-adjusted compounding on a $1,000 starting stake."
}
```

Sources:
